verses.commentary: Disciples' question: 'Who is greatest in the kingdom?' The fourth Matthean discourse begins — the community discourse. Pride about ranking surfaces in the inner circle.
Jesus calls a child to himself, places child among them. A live visual aid. Children in first-century Mediterranean culture were socially marginal — useful for kingdom inversion.

'Unless you turn (straphete, στραφῆτε) and become like children, you will never enter the kingdom.' Not merely greatness — entry itself requires the child-like turn. Adult competence is the wrong direction.
Greatness = humbling like the child. The child does not strive for status; the disciple must imitate that posture. Greatness inverted.
Receiving a child in Jesus' name = receiving Jesus. The vulnerable made into the locus of Christ's presence. Care for the small is care for the Messiah.

'Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to stumble (skandalisai, σκανδαλίσῃ)' — better drowned with a millstone (mylos onikos, μύλος ὀνικός — donkey-driven millstone, massive). Severity of judgment for spiritual child-abuse.
Woe to the world for stumbling blocks. Stumbling blocks must come — fallen world produces them — but woe to those through whom. Inevitability does not excuse agency.
If hand or foot causes stumbling, cut off. Eternal fire vs. maimed entry to life. Same hyperbole as 5:30. Sin's seriousness justifies radical excision.
If eye causes stumbling, gouge out. The repetition emphasizes that nothing — not even valued faculties — is worth keeping if it costs the soul.

'Do not despise one of these little ones — their angels in heaven always see the face of my Father.' Guardian angels with direct access to the Father. Despising children is despising what God protects.
[Textual variant absent from earliest manuscripts. Later manuscripts add: 'For the Son of Man came to save what was lost' (compare Luke 19:10). Scribal harmonization.]
Hundred sheep, one strays. Will the shepherd not leave the ninety-nine and seek the one? The math seems wrong but the heart explains it.
If found — rejoicing more over the one than over the ninety-nine. Recovery joy outstrips routine satisfaction. The shepherd's heart skews toward the lost.
'Not the will of your Father that one of these little ones perish.' Divine intent applied to vulnerable disciples. The shepherd parable interprets ecclesial care.
'If your brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault between you and him alone.' Private confrontation first. Wins your brother — the goal is restoration, not exposure.
If he does not listen, take one or two others — Deuteronomy 19:15 standard (every matter established by two or three witnesses). Escalation is procedural, not punitive.

If still refuses — tell the church (ekklesia, ἐκκλησία). Final step: the gathered community. If refuses the church — let him be to you as Gentile and tax collector. Note: but Jesus dined with tax collectors. The 'excommunication' is itself missional posture.
Binding and loosing extended to the community (compare 16:19 to Peter). Church decisions on discipline carry heavenly weight. Authority shared, not centralized.
If two agree on earth about anything they ask — done by my Father in heaven. The corporate prayer-pact.
'Where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I in the midst of them.' Smallest congregation has Christ's presence. The shekinah relocates from temple to community gathered in his name.
Peter's question: 'How often shall I forgive my brother — seven times?' Rabbinic limit was three. Peter doubles it and adds one — seemingly generous.

'Not seven, but seventy times seven' (hebdomekontakis hepta, ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά — or 'seventy-seven,' echoing Lamech in Genesis 4:24 inverted). Unlimited forgiveness, undoing Cain's escalation. The kingdom reverses violence's math.
Parable: kingdom like a king settling accounts with servants. Sets up the lesson on forgiveness given and required.
Ten thousand talents (myrios talanton, μύρια τάλαντα) — an unimaginable amount. A talent was ~6,000 denarii (20 years' wages); ten thousand talents could exceed national budgets. Hyperbolic debt.
Cannot pay. Master orders him, wife, children, possessions sold — debt slavery. Just demand under ancient law.
Servant falls, begs patience — promises to repay all. The promise is empty; the debt is unpayable.
Master's compassion (splagchnistheis, σπλαγχνισθεὶς) — releases him and forgives the debt. Total cancellation. Mercy exceeds the request.
Same servant finds fellow servant who owes a hundred denarii (100 days' wages — real money but trivial against ten thousand talents). Seizes him by the throat: 'Pay what you owe.'
Fellow servant falls and begs — identical words to verse 26: 'Be patient with me.' The parallel exposes the moral asymmetry.
Refuses, throws him into prison. The recipient of immense grace withholds minor grace. Heart unchanged by grace received.
Fellow servants distressed (lypeo, λυπέω) — report to master. The community recognizes the injustice and protests.
Master: 'Wicked servant, I forgave you all that debt because you begged me.' The mercy was real and proportional to the request.
'Should not you have had mercy on your fellow servant, as I had mercy on you?' Reciprocity required. Receiving mercy without extending mercy is the original betrayal.
Angry master delivers him to torturers until full payment — i.e., forever, since payment is impossible. The unforgiving forfeit their forgiveness.

'So also my heavenly Father will do to every one of you, if you do not forgive your brother from your heart.' The application is direct: forgiveness from the heart, not as gesture. Without horizontal forgiveness, vertical forgiveness ceases. Compare 6:14-15.
